speaker: interviewer
text: We're still interviewing Mr. Walter Rockler on 21 March, 1996 in the District of Columbia. I'm still Bruce Einhorn. Mr. Rockler, you were talking about the bank cases.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, although my original notion, as I told you, was to go there and do a reasonable amount of work, and coast, and see Europe, I wound up working, I would say, an average of 60 to 80 hours a week, which is more than I've ever done in private practice, although I had times when I averaged 60. And in fact, from time to time, during the winter of '47, and in the winter of '48, I remember sleeping on the stone floors of the courthouse office that I had at Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you have any staff for these cases?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had interpreters I had primarily Otto Werber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he good?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was extremely good. But I'll get into it later, history or subsequently.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was as good as any of the staff people, the administrative people. I also did a lot of traveling. I spent a lot of time in Prague on business because the Dresdner Bank had taken over a very large chain of Czech banks, one of the largest, the [NON-ENGLISH] banks. And they've been active in the Sudeten territories, which the Germans took over. And I had lots of business trips to Prague, and I got to like it. Prague was only four hours by car from Nuremberg, reasonably-- Nuremberg's reasonably close to the Czech border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who is in charge of the area of Prague where you did your investigating?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Communist party. There was a communist government by February '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any problem getting in and out and doing your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were two phases in Czechoslovakia. Up till February '48, the government-- I'm trying to remember who the key Czech official was, Masaryk. I think his son was active-- committed suicide, allegedly, or was thrown out of a window in a very popular Eastern European sport called defenestration. When I first went to Prague, it was a flourishing city and a hell of a lot nicer than it is today. It wasn't damaged during the war. It had been occupied by the Germans before the war, March of '39. There was virtually no damage anywhere in the city. It was a very low level economy, but they were producing. They were better off than most of Central Europe. But what was most refreshing about the place was it wasn't damaged. It just wasn't rubble like Vienna, or Budapest, or any other city you could mention. And I liked the Czechs. When you went to Prague, although it was a bilingual city, both German and Czech, the Czechs would not respond to anything spoken in German. Obviously, I didn't speak any Czech, so I was limited to English there. But you couldn't ask a question in German. By this time, I was reasonably fluent in German. I'd been there, let's say, a year or so. Today, you go to Prague, you can't ask a question in Russian, but they'll freely respond to German, since the Germans own the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the trips to Prague fruitful from an investigative perspective?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would say so. I dealt with a-- I dealt with a-- with a Czech banker whose name was [? [NON-ENGLISH], ?] little [NON-ENGLISH], who had been a minister in various Czech governments after the war, generally agrarian and right wing, but I think-- and I got to know him fairly well. He was the chairman of the board of a bigger Czech chain called the [NON-ENGLISH] banks, and I did deal with him. We collected records there. We did the same thing in Holland too. I spent three weeks in the basement of a Dutch bank collecting records. I went to Belgium and talked to-- again, in Holland, I'm trying to remember. Oh, yeah. I went to [NON-ENGLISH] and talked to the Philips officials, Philips being a big radio communications firm, who were suspected of collaborating with the Germans anyhow. I talked to various people in Holland and Belgium. I went to Paris fairly often, but that was not on business. Maybe every two months I'd go there because my sister was going to school there. She was at the Sorbonne. And similarly, at a couple points I went to London because she had moved from the Sorbonne to Oxford. So those were kind of five-day vacations which I would periodically take. But Prague was a mix in the course of business and sometimes just a few days off because you get there quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how much time did you spend investigating the bank cases before the trial began?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year. A year. Not only the bank case, all the cases. I had the Reichsbank, the German Ministry of Economics, which-- the Dresdner Bank was closely tied to my ministry of economics defendant. They did business together. And when necessary, they would invoke the services of the Gestapo or the SD. They were very helpful in negotiating with people who were not friendly to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us-- tell us what the SD was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what the SD was.

speaker: interviewee
text: The SD was a kind of a police branch of the SS, literally translated in German as Sicherheitsdienst, which means security service of the SS. The SS itself was called the Schutzstaffel. That's what the SS comes from, and that means the defense group. It was a party group, and came into existence before the Nazis seized political power. In other words, it was a selected bunch of thugs, and of those thugs, the SD was even more thuggish. The SD was probably a little bit worse than the Gestapo, whose reputation is well founded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say there was a connection between the bankers and financiers and the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'll give you a concrete illustration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Baron Louis Rothschild was the head of the Paris branch of the Rothschild family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, he was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time, he wasn't so Jewish. I mean, the Rothschilds had intermarried for so many generations since Napoleon that he was maybe a quarter, or a fourth, or an eighth-- an eighth Jewish, and had, of course, a title of French nobility, like a fair number of Jews in Europe-- German nobility. But the Germans seized Louis Rothschild in Paris. The Rothschild family owned one of the most modern steel processing plants in Czechoslovakia, which was on the Polish border, known as Vitkovice-- very modern. The Germans, of course, seized it as soon as they entered Czechoslovakia, but they wanted legal title. That, they could only get-- the Germans liked legal titles. They weren't content merely to grab. They liked to force the owners to turn stuff over to them. They had him in a Paris hotel, and both Rasche and Kehrl, Ministry of Economics guy, were negotiating with him. And I put the word 'negotiating' in quotes because one of their negotiating techniques was to invoke the Gestapo. Louis Rothschild wasn't a total fool. He had a pretty good idea what his negotiating position was. I think he finally made a deal to turn over the stock certificates to the Vitkovice plant-- which is what the Germans wanted, what the Dresdner Bank wanted-- in exchange for getting out of the country alive. And it was a remarkable transaction, not in that the Germans got the plant, but he actually did get out alive. That was what was remarkable. Germans were not all that good in keeping faith on these kinds of contracts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to meet Rothschild?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had no interest in Rothschild. He was an episode in my case. There were similar transactions with certain Czech Jews who had large holdings in the Sudeten area of Czechoslovakia. The [NON-ENGLISH] and [GERMAN] families were notorious for owning large tracts of soft coal, so-called brown coal. They were very well to do. They were Jews, but they were very Germanized too. I mean, this was a German section of Czechoslovakia. German was their language. Their contacts were all German. They knew that they were in good standing during the Nazi period, though, and these families ultimately wound up in New York City, getting some money out because they negotiated more favorably with some of the Nazi hierarchy. I think particularly, they knew Goering or someone. So they came out alive and with some property.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what exactly were the legal charges that were leveled against your defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: The primary charge against the Dresdner Bank was grabbing Jewish properties before or after the owners were murdered, you know, accessories to murder and theft. These are war crimes under the Hague Conventions, particularly for properties outside of Germany, which was the real focus-- Czech properties, Dutch property, French property.

speaker: interviewee
text: A second charge had nothing to do with Jews because they did exactly the same thing with Dutch, Belgians, and Czechs. They didn't care who owned the property. It was the property they wanted. Why did the Dresdner Bank want the property? They were middlemen. They would bird-dog properties. In other words, they'd say, this is something we ought to grab, and deal with Kehrl in the Ministry of Economics, who would say yes. Why did they want to bird dog it? First, they were all Nazi patriots. Second, when the properties were grabbed by Germans, they'd be placed with clients of the banks, and that was very desirable from the standpoint of making money.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I say, this was both part of the so-called Aryanization program and also part of German occupation policy. The Germans had the notion that when they had successfully conquered all of Europe and all the world, they wanted to own it as well in the most direct sense. They had an economic policy called Verflechtung, which means interlacing. Interlacing meant taking over and Germans controlling. It was a rather euphemistic term for grabbing. This was-- it's all prohibited by traditional war crimes law, prohibited by the Hague Conventions very directly. The Dresdner Bank was known in Germany as the SS bank, which was a very favorable reputation in Germany. And one of their other activities was making loans, interest free or an extremely low interest rate for building concentration camps. Building concentration camps required money. They furnished money. That charge did not stick. The first charge stuck. American, American judges would not find anything wrong in a bank making a low-interest loan. You know, business is business. They thought this was too close to traditional banking business, the financing of concentration camps. In other words, if an American bank did it, it was OK too. The vice president of the Reichsbank was charged with something different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emil Puhl, P-U-H-L. He was one of Schacht's deputies, Schacht having been acquitted. But Puhl, I think, was in charge of running the vaults of the Reichsbank, which were filled with gold teeth, gold wedding rings, gold fountain pens, gold wrist watches, any form of gold that could be taken off the form of-- off the bodies of corpses in extermination camps-- Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor, and so forth. That was his specialty. And these things weren't mounds. They were mountains of gold. You know, you took enough gold teeth off bodies, you had a lot of gold teeth. And among other things, Emil Puhl admitted in testimony to visiting concentration camps, going to the source, and he was convicted of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were all of your defendants tried together, or was it-- were they tried separately?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all tried-- these were all separate trials. The Reichsbank charges and the Dresdner Bank charges, except in a very general sense, didn't have anything in common. The Dresdner Bank did not turn over gold tooth to the Reichsbank. The Reichsbank, being like the Federal Reserve system, was the central bank of issue. It was the German equivalent of the Bank of England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a separate trial for the economic ministry?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all one trial, but it was episodic. They were-- I think there must have been something like 25 separate defendants, and for all practical purposes, most of these cases were separate cases within, within the framework of what was called the Ministries Case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that mean you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ministries Case was a catch-all case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Case 11, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Case 11. The primary defendant who got all public attention was Ernst von Weizsacker, who was a deputy foreign minister under von Ribbentrop. His was a notable case. His son, Richard von Weizsacker, was president of the West German Republic for years and years and years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you prosecute the elder von Weizsacker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had nothing to do with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Kempner was in charge of that, and I think by title may have been overall in charge of the whole Ministries Case, although he had absolutely nothing to do with my case. Nobody had anything to do with my case. I had no supervision. The case was the case that I conceived and brought. I shouldn't say that I conceived because I certainly didn't conceive it. Foster Adams conceived it. Rawlings Ragland worked on the overall outlines, or Goldberg worked on it. But I wound up executing the conception, and I probably prepared 800 to 1,000 documents for presentation and trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this Dr. Kempner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was Kempner? He was a German refugee, not Jewish, who had been state's attorney, whatever you want to call him, for the-- for Prussia before the Nazi accession to power, before '33. It is my understanding, although I didn't have that much contact with him-- I knew him, but we certainly weren't close. We were generations apart. He must have been every bit of 40 years old, which I thought was pretty damn old at this time. And I think he stayed in his office for the first year of the Nazi regime, into '34.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Prussia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prussia. Was obviously out of favor because he wasn't a Nazi in any sense. He was I think neither a party member nor any kind of affiliate of the Nazis-- pretty good fellow, as far as I know. I don't know anything the matter with him. And finally, he came to the United States and lived somewhere in Pennsylvania for the rest of his life. But he obviously was knowledgeable about German affairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said he was on the von Weizsacker case.

speaker: interviewee
text: He handled the von Weizsacker case, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Did you ever work with him on any case?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't work with anybody hardly at all anywhere. I worked more outside on my own bailiwick with a fellow named Charlie Lyons-- Lyon, without an S-- who was a tax lawyer from Washington, probably responsible for my subsequent career.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to work with Mr. Lyon?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just got along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Nuremberg prosecutor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He was a-- he was, I think, the principal prosecutor on the Flick case, which was a case against a German Nazi steel magnate who was convicted along with three or four of his colleagues in the Flick steelworks and who had a great career after the war. He later became the owner of Mercedes-Benz, Daimler-Benz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the case where you said you occasionally put in some time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We, we overlapped in one respect. Rasche was a member of the so-called Himmler kreis-- kreis being the German word meaning circle, which we translated as friends of Himmler. And Lyons and Flick, I think, also had connections. And there were some other people-- Keppler, Wilhelm Keppler, who was in and around the economic of party affairs. And Lyons was working up some kind of charges against the members of the circle of Himmler. And my defendant, Rasche, was a part of or on the fringes of that circle, so we overlapped, and I got to know Charlie Lyon very well. And we were on very good terms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you prepare the indictments against your defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe-- I know-- I might have work on them. I just don't remember that clearly. I know that nobody said prepare an indictment. It was a piece of an overall indictment for the whole Ministries Case, which ran on page after page.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your share of the Ministries Cases, your bank cases, actually begin in court?

speaker: interviewee
text: Late-- I'm trying to remember because it's getting a little hazy at this point. But I think it was the latter part of 1948 that the cases actually began in court, although we had had what amounted to kind of deposition hearings, commission hearings of witnesses long before that, probably beginning in the spring of '48, you know, off an on. The defense would propose to produce some witnesses, and we'd have a hearing before some court commissioner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what would be done with the records of those proceedings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were useful in the trial record. They were in effect kind of sub-testimony in the case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did your cases run consecutively or concurrently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Consecutively.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did they run?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I must have been two, three weeks in court.

speaker: interviewer
text: For all of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: For all of them. The actual court proceedings were a very small part of my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the court proceedings were undertaken, how much time did you actually spend in the courtrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: All day. I remember spending three, four days cross-examining Rasche, and maybe a couple days with Kehrl, and appearing there for episodic appearances. I also participated in lots of commission hearings, I mean, I would say at least 12, 15 of those. I don't remember the exact number. They were mostly of defense witnesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the nature of your cross-examine of Rasche?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clumsy, but effective. I didn't know anything about trial tactics or finesse or grace. Rasche was a terrible liar. It was built into his soul. Incidentally, I should say his defense counsel was an able lawyer who had defended von Papen in the big case and gotten von Papen off on multiple--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us again who von Papen was.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Egon Kubuschok.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the lawyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the German defense counsel for Rasche.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us, if you would, who the first client of this lawyer, von Papen was, the gentleman who was acquitted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Franz von Papen was the head of the Catholic Party in Germany, which, when Hitler became chancellor, had thrown in the Catholic Party vote to Hitler, and thereby became deputy chancellor when Hitler became chancellor in '33. He fell out of favor a little bit later and wound up only as ambassador to Austria before the Germans grabbed Austria, and he contributed to the taking over of Austria. But he was acquitted in the major trial. He was one of the three people who were acquitted. Three people were acquitted before the IMT. One, of course, was Schacht, who was the president of the Reichsbank. The other was von Papen, who was deputy chancellor at one point under Hitler. And the third was a operative in the ministry of propaganda in Goebbels's operation by the name of Fritzsche, Hans Fritzsche. Don't ask me to spell Fritzsche.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're saying the lawyer for von Papen then later became the lawyer for--

speaker: interviewee
text: For Rasche and Dresdner Bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a good lawyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a good lawyer and as far as I know was a pretty good man too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a Nazi, as far as you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and that was very unusual. Kubuschok was a guy who had practiced law in Berlin before the Nazis. He was a rather competent, able trial lawyer. That was his business. He was with one of the Berlin firms, and I don't know the name of it, but I wouldn't be surprised if, pre-Nazi days, it may not have had one or another Jewish member. I think he had Catholic affiliations, Kubuschok, because he represented von Papen, who was a big Catholic leader in Germany. But Kubuschok was low-keyed, and he never made any of the major kind of Nazi defenses like there's no law that governs us. You're another bastard just like us, which was one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the thrust of his defense?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the thrust of his defense of Rasche?

speaker: interviewee
text: Concentrate on the facts. And I was on pretty good terms with him. We'd kibbutz in the corridors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give us an example of what you mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember talking to him about the case at one point. He said, oh, you've got a terrible case, he said. But if you knew what I knew, you'd have a good case. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he spoke pretty good English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you, you said some--

speaker: interviewee
text: To illustrate the kind of relationship I had with him, we-- there was some good-looking German girl who showed up in our courtroom all the time. I say our courtroom. These were mainly commission hearings. Whenever there was anything going on, she'd be there, and my assumption was that she was a, a relative of the defendant. You know, why else would anybody want to listen to turgid testimony about banking operations?

speaker: interviewer
text: So at one recess, I said, who's the blonde back there? He said, oh, you want to meet her? I'll introduce you at the next recess, and he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was a rather nice girl, good looking, who was not Nazi at all. She was a-- she was actually an Austrian news reporter covering the trial for some Viennese paper, but she was German, not Austrian. She was from Berlin. Her family was Nazi inside and out. She had a sister who was married to Rommel's adjutant, a major in the German army, who was also a leader of the Bundesdeutsche Madchen, which was the Nazi girls organization, equivalent of the Girl Scouts here, except Nazi. Her father was a German industrialist who was a Nazi. She was a great-granddaughter of a chancellor of Prussia, von Hardenberg, at the time of Napoleon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was very leery of her because I figured, what the hell? I don't need anybody pilfering my papers in the evening. But I followed her reporting career with interest. She was quite anti-Nazi, which was, given her family background, kind of unusual. But she had another aspect to her background. She had been married to an Englishman who died in internment in the first year of the war. That did not contribute to making her Nazi. Now, did I hear you just say that Kuperschmidt's lack of affiliation with national socialism was unusual for defense council in the cases?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I would say he felt it was unusual for any German lawyer. My guess is no more than 5% of the German bar stayed out of the Nazi Lawyers Guild, and as far as I know, Kubuschok was not a member of it. As far as I know, he was also not a Nazi party member. And of the defense counsel, I would say 99%-- in fact, he's the only one I know who wouldn't have been. And I may be even wrong on that. I may be giving him too much credit, but that's as much as I could ever find out about him. He also had a very obscure career under the Nazis. He moved to East Germany, somewhere near Dresden. As far as I know, represented prisoners of war, DPs, slave labor, probably never won a case in four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did Rasche make any admissions in the course of your examination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Rasche was an unmitigated, unlimited liar. And as I said, my cross-examination was pretty detailed. I had folders on all kinds of connections to Rasche-- I mean, maybe 10, 20 documents under the names of various persons he did business with. But my technique consisted of saying, do you know Mr. X? And he'd say no, no, I never heard of Mr. X.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd have my file on Mr. X next to me. I'd say, did you ever have dinner with Mr. X? He said, no, how can I have dinner with him? I didn't even know him. And so I'd say-- hand him a piece of paper which had an identification number, and I'd say, is that your signature at the bottom of this letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he'd look at it for a while. He'd say, yeah, it looks like mine. And then I'd have him read the thing. We had simultaneous translation there. You know, he'd read it in German. It'd come out in English for the judges and for me. And the letter would be, Dear Mr. X, it's a pleasure to see you again. I've enjoyed our many meetings and dinners for the last year, and we ought to get together and talk some more about your taking over Z plant or whatever. And give my regards to your very nice wife and so forth, you know. And I'd have maybe 10 letters like that, so perfectly obvious he knew Mr. X rather thoroughly. And this would go on day after day. Kubuschok would come out at recess, and start pulling out his hair. He said, this stupid son of a bitch is going to convict himself, which he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is what his own lawyer said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, you know, I mean, he, he didn't see how you could win a case after your defendant had been identified with about 12, 15, 30 lies, point blank, and I thought contributed very greatly to my case. I-- once I latched onto this technique, that's what my cross-examination consisted of, just leading him into one lie after another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cross-examine any of the other defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cross-examined them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the other cross examines-- examinations similar in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to some extent they were. They weren't quite as heavily documented as my case against Rasche or Kehrl. That was-- that was my nucleus case. That's what I started out with. That's where I put my efforts, but Puhl is interesting, the bank vice president.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Reichsbank.

speaker: interviewee
text: Reichsbank, yeah. Because he alleged that he was a defender and patron of one or another Jew, but he also denied knowing anything about Nazi atrocities against Jews. At the same time, he stressed how useful he had been in saving these Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, if you don't know of any atrocities, what are you saving them from? You know, there's a certain inherent contradiction there. That, plus the fact that he visited all the famous concentration camps and that he had mounds of teeth and similar assorted personal items taken from corpses made him look like something of a liar. Let me change tapes before we continue. Thank you.
